item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is is able to eat anything other than hair? as if, say, someone gave the scp a patch of skin that was removed by a dear friend of the person, would it accept that? or would that be ignored?

2225 would probably be a little grossed out. It would not eat that.

this is the top article that appears when you search "hair snake" on the wiki.

Huh! Indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is is able to eat anything other than hair? as if, say, someone gave the scp a patch of skin that was removed by a dear friend of the person, would it accept that? or would that be ignored?

Edit: as a side (but a wee bit random) note, this is the top article that appears when you search "hair snake" on the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(sorry, slight necropost.)

You're fine here, GE Smith. Necroposting is just replying to an old thread without adding anything that furthers the conversation, like "lol" or "I agree" that people can't really respond to.

There's nothing wrong with posting a reply in old SCP discussion threads so long as you're contributing something meaningful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Especially in an organization that hordes rare and priceless things and fiercely protects them?

(sorry, slight necropost.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good catch. Tags added. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't this be tagged "sapient" and "sentient"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was already hovering over the upvote button, but the interview did it for me. Amazing work, as usual Zyn. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun fact: lintworm is the Dutch word for tapeworm. Also, not voting because while this is a fine article, it doesn't do a lot for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kiryu is a city (桐生市) and a surname. I picked it because Kiryu was my favorite Yu-Gi-Oh card when I was little.

Dr. Mark Kiryu is actually a recurring character. He's got his own set of labs for anomalous objects testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Proof that SCPs can make you go D'awww. Also, Kiryu is Japanese for 'mechanical dragon', I believe, or maybe that's just the Godzilla talking. Nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: D'awww. I want a hair-eating, silver Lindwyrm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's not really much connection to the legend per se, it was just the inspiration that made me decide to make the creature a lindworm (in the prelim drafts, it was a near-featureless humanoid with metallic skin). Lindworm fit better with the eating parts of people and stealing shiny things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No lie, you didn't have me up until those last two lines, but damn. I mean, the effects of what it does are really interesting, but until the very end, I was hovering over a novote. Honestly, I don't see the connection to that legend you linked to, but it doesn't really matter because this totally works on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awww.. what a cute, hair eating, child dragon thing!
Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! I've fixed up the grammar and rearranged part of the containment procedures to make it flow a bit better. Let me know if there are any other issues you find; I definitely accidentally'd a word with that "have found to lead" bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Plus One. I'm trying to think of what the SCP would produce with me and some of my old friends… (me Korax, me friend!!!)

Just some nitpicking on the text, please tell me if I'm wrong:

''and waiting for SCP-2225 to approach the speaker on its own have found to lead to successful interactions.''
I think the correct grammar would be ''…was found to…''?

In the Procedure, why not always use the same pair of friends? Does it have to be a different pair for every meal? This is not clear.

''Nevertheless, all subjects report that all speech…''
I would suggest ''…in every instance, subjects report that the speech…'' (I just don't like the repetition of the word ''all'')

''currently housed there.''
I suggest: ''..currently housed therewith''

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weird in a creative way, and a nice mystery. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely marvelous. Great when in chat, still great now. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kudos for taking weirdness to a whole new level!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article was good, the interview takes it beyond good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's basically got the mentality of a kid though. There's still room for an elder dragon lord.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welp, it seems Zyn has beaten me to the punch as far as writing a sapient dragon SCP loosely based on mythology is concerned. GG.

item.title: Author Notes
item.content:encoded:
First off, many, many thanks to all these nice people who reviewed the varying draft stages for me in chat: Cimmerian, Live2Die, Ireul, Baby_boo, Proxima, lfairy, MisterFlames, theworstninja, Drewbear (helped with phrasing), SoundChaser, Armstrong, TwistedGears, Light, ihp/ihpkmn, Nanoro, Decibelle, Vince, Dmatix, and TroyL and Soulless who both did quite thorough readings.

The inspiration for this originally came from the Prince Lindworm story; a very nice version of said story can be read here. Eventually I did some more reading up on lindworms, and there are quite a few tales that involve them interacting with humans (though usually someone ended up dead in the end >.>).

I’ve always thought it was fascinating how mythological/fairytale creatures sometimes have very specific diets, and I remembered reading somewhere that a few centuries ago friends would exchange locks of hair. I was thinking of how to make the Lindworm eat people without harming people, and not only is hair crazy symbolic/significant in history (check it out, Wikipedia article on Locks of hair), but it’s more romantic and easily-obtained than say, toenails.
